description: Baptism is understood as effecting forgiveness/new life.
Single bishop presiding over a local church in unity.
Authority transmitted through episcopal succession from the apostles.
Christ is identified with the divine Logos (Word) who existed before creation and became incarnate; draws on both Jewish Wisdom tradition and Stoic/Platonic Logos philosophy.
The Eucharist is the actual body and blood of Christ rather than a mere symbol; Ignatius calls it "the flesh of our Savior Jesus Christ."
Christ only appeared to have a human body; his suffering and death were illusory; the divine could not truly suffer. Condemned by Ignatius of Antioch and later by the whole proto-orthodox tradition.
Summary of core Christian beliefs used as a hermeneutical standard and identity marker; precursor to formal creeds; varies in wording by author but consistent in content.
Martyrdom is understood as the supreme Christian witness; the martyr participates in Christ's own passion and gains direct entry to eternal life; shapes identity and ethics of the early church.

Two distinct gods: the inferior, wrathful creator-god of the Hebrew Bible (demiurge) and the higher, loving God revealed by Jesus; the Hebrew Bible is wholly rejected; only an edited Pauline corpus and Luke are canonical.
resolution: Condemned; Marcionites excommunicated; drove proto-orthodox canon formation

description: The process of determining which writings carry authoritative apostolic status; accelerated by Marcion's radical reduction and by Gnostic use of non-apostolic texts; informal consensus by mid-3rd century, formal by late 4th century.
resolution: Substantially resolved by end of 4th century (Athanasius Festal Letter 367)

description: Salvation is achieved through secret knowledge (gnosis) of one's divine origin; the material world is the creation of an inferior demiurge; the divine spark within the elect must return to the Pleroma (divine fullness).

Claims of ongoing ecstatic prophecy by Montanus, Priscilla, and Maximilla as the direct voice of the Paraclete; strict asceticism; imminent parousia; challenged the normativity of episcopal authority.
resolution: Condemned as heresy; movement persisted into 6th century

description: Irenaeus' doctrine that Christ recapitulates (sums up and reverses) Adam's disobedience at every stage of life; as Adam fell through disobedience, Christ restores through obedience; key anti-gnostic argument.
Jesus was a mere man who was adopted as Son of God at his baptism (or resurrection); defends divine monarchy against ditheism; associated with Theodotus of Byzantium and Paul of Samosata.
resolution: Condemned; Council of Antioch (268) deposed Paul of Samosata; Council of Nicaea (325) condemned Adoptionism

description: The Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are three successive modes or manifestations of one divine person rather than three distinct persons; defends divine unity but conflates the persons.
resolution: Condemned; Sabellius excommunicated by Callixtus I of Rome c.220

description: Dispute whether baptism administered by schismatics/heretics is valid; Cyprian of Carthage held it invalid and required re-baptism; Rome under Stephen I held heretical baptism valid; dispute never fully resolved in antiquity.
resolution: Partially resolved: Rome's position became dominant in the West; Eastern tradition more varied

description: Belief in a literal future 1,000-year earthly reign of Christ based on Revelation 20; held by Justin Martyr, Irenaeus, Papias, and Tertullian; later rejected by Origen and Dionysius of Alexandria on exegetical grounds.
resolution: Marginalized in mainstream orthodoxy by 4th century; not formally condemned

description: Honor given to martyrs and saints as intercessors before God; includes memorial feasts at tombs, keeping relics, invoking saints by name, and celebrating anniversary martyrdom dates; the earliest clear attestation is the Martyrdom of Polycarp (c.155-160 CE), which describes preserving Polycarp's bones as "more valuable than precious stones" and celebrating his death-anniversary; rooted in the Roman refrigerium (funerary meal) and the broader culture of honouring heroic dead.
resolution: Formally endorsed in Catholic and Orthodox tradition; contested in Protestant Reformation; not condemned in antiquity

description: The title Theotokos (Greek: "God-bearer" / "Mother of God") applied to the Virgin Mary; implies that the child she bore was truly divine, not merely human; first attested in Origen (c.230 CE, Homily on Luke) and the Sub Tuum Praesidium papyrus (c.250-350 CE, the oldest known Marian prayer); became a decisive flashpoint when Nestorius of Constantinople objected to the title in 428, sparking the Nestorian controversy.
resolution: Council of Ephesus (431) formally confirmed Theotokos; Nestorius condemned and deposed

description: The use of visual representations of Christ, Mary, and saints in Christian worship and devotion; earliest surviving examples in the Roman catacombs (Good Shepherd, Orans figure, Fractio Panis, c.150-200 CE) and the Dura-Europos house-church (c.230 CE, earliest surviving cycle of Christian narrative painting); debated throughout antiquity as to whether images were objects of veneration or merely didactic aids; the Council of Elvira (c.306) canon 36 prohibited images in churches.
resolution: Major controversy in Byzantine Iconoclasm (726-843); images affirmed at Second Council of Nicaea (787); remain central in Catholic and Orthodox practice

description: The formal doctrine that Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are three co-equal, co-eternal persons of one divine substance (homoousios, "of the same essence"); the Greek term Trias (Trinity) first used theologically by Theophilus of Antioch (c.180 CE); the Latin formulation (Trinitas, tres personae, una substantia) by Tertullian (c.213 CE); definitively formulated at the Council of Nicaea (325) against Arian subordinationism and completed at Constantinople (381).
resolution: Affirmed at Council of Nicaea (325) and Constantinople (381); remains the foundational doctrine of Catholic, Orthodox, and most Protestant Christianity

description: The doctrine associated with Arius of Alexandria (c.256-336) that the Son of God is a created being—the first and greatest creature, brought into existence from nothing by the Father before all ages—and therefore not co-eternal or co-equal with the Father; the Son is "like" the Father but not homoousios (of the same substance); expressed in Arius's Thalia (c.318 CE); Arianism had widespread episcopal support in the early 4th century, including from Eusebius of Nicomedia.
resolution: Condemned at Council of Nicaea (325); persisted in homoian and homoiousian forms; survived among Germanic tribes (Visigoths, Vandals, Lombards) until the 7th century; finally marginalized in the West by the Council of Toledo (589)
